content:
# Acts Chapter 20 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 250-251

---

**1** And after the uproar had ceased, Paul, having sent for the disciples and having exhorted them, embraced them and departed to go into Macedonia.

**2** And having passed through those regions and having exhorted them with many words, he came into Greece.

**3** And having spent three months there, when a plot was formed against him by the Jews as he was about to set sail for Syria, he decided to return through Macedonia.

**4** And there accompanied him Sopater son of Pyrrhus, a Berean; and of the Thessalonians, Aristarchus and Secundus; and Gaius of Derbe, and Timothy; and of Asia, Tychicus and Trophimus.

**5** These, having gone ahead, were waiting for us at Troas.

**6** And we sailed from Philippi after the days of Unleavened Bread, and came to them at Troas within five days, where we stayed seven days.

**7** And on the first day of the week, when we had gathered together to break bread, Paul was speaking to them, being about to depart on the next day, and he prolonged his speech until midnight.

**8** And there were many lamps in the upper room where we were gathered together.

**9** And a certain young man named Eutychus, sitting in the window, was being overcome by deep sleep as Paul continued speaking at length; and having been overcome by sleep, he fell from the third story and was taken up dead.

**10** But Paul went down and fell upon him, and having embraced him said, "Do not be troubled, for his life is in him."

**11** And having gone up and having broken bread and eaten, and having conversed at length until dawn, so he departed.

**12** And they brought the boy alive, and were greatly comforted.

**13** And we, having gone ahead to the ship, set sail for Assos, intending from there to take Paul on board; for so he had arranged, intending himself to go on foot.

**14** And when he met us at Assos, we took him on board and came to Mitylene.

**15** And having sailed from there, on the following day we arrived opposite Chios; and the next day we crossed over to Samos; and the day after we came to Miletus.

**16** For Paul had decided to sail past Ephesus, so that he might not have to spend time in Asia; for he was hastening, if it were possible for him, to be in Jerusalem on the day of Pentecost.

**17** And from Miletus he sent to Ephesus and summoned the elders of the church.

**18** And when they had come to him, he said to them: "You yourselves know, from the first day on which I set foot in Asia, how I was with you the whole time,

**19** serving the Lord with all humility and with tears and with trials that came upon me through the plots of the Jews;

**20** how I did not shrink from declaring to you anything that was profitable, and teaching you publicly and from house to house,

**21** testifying both to Jews and to Greeks of repentance toward God and of faith in our Lord Jesus Christ.

**22** "And now, behold, I am going to Jerusalem, bound in the Spirit, not knowing what will happen to me there,

**23** except that the Holy Spirit testifies to me in every city, saying that bonds and afflictions await me.

**24** But I consider my life of no account as precious to myself, so that I may finish my course and the ministry which I received from the Lord Jesus, to testify to the gospel of the grace of God.

**25** "And now, behold, I know that all of you, among whom I went about proclaiming the kingdom, will see my face no more.

**26** Therefore I testify to you on this day that I am innocent of the blood of all,

**27** for I did not shrink from declaring to you the whole counsel of God.

**28** Take heed to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has placed you as overseers, to shepherd the church of God, which he obtained through the blood of his own.

**29** I know that after my departure fierce wolves will come in among you, not sparing the flock;

**30** and from among your own selves men will arise, speaking distorted things, to draw away the disciples after themselves.

**31** Therefore be watchful, remembering that for three years, night and day, I did not cease admonishing each one with tears.

**32** "And now I commend you to God and to the word of his grace, which is able to build you up and to give you the inheritance among all those who have been sanctified.

**33** I coveted no one's silver or gold or clothing.

**34** You yourselves know that these hands ministered to my own needs and to those who were with me.

**35** In all things I showed you that by laboring in this way it is necessary to help the weak, and to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, that he himself said, 'It is more blessed to give than to receive.'"

**36** And having said these things, he knelt down with them all and prayed.

**37** And there was much weeping among them all, and they fell upon Paul's neck and kissed him,

**38** grieving most of all over the word that he had spoken, that they would see his face no more. And they accompanied him to the ship.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 20 divides into three movements: Paul's journey through Macedonia and Greece (vv. 1-6), the Troas incident with Eutychus (vv. 7-12), and the voyage to Miletus with Paul's farewell address to the Ephesian elders (vv. 13-38). The farewell discourse (vv. 18-35) is the only speech in Acts addressed to a Christian audience, and it carries the weight of a pastoral testament.

The Sinaiticus text is well-preserved for this chapter across pages 250-251. Three translators achieved broad agreement on the narrative sections, diverging most in Paul's farewell speech where theological and rhetorical nuances created varied English renderings.

The most theologically significant textual decision is v. 28, where Sinaiticus reads "church of God" (ΕΚΚΛΗΣΙΑΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ) rather than "church of the Lord." The phrase "through the blood of his own" (ΔΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΑΙΜΑΤΟΣ ΤΟΥ ΙΔΙΟΥ) is preserved in its ambiguity -- likely meaning "through the blood of his Own [One]," i.e., his Son. The consensus renders this as "the blood of his own" to hold the ambiguity present in the Greek.

Verse 25 preserves the shorter Sinaiticus reading "the kingdom" without "of God," which all three translators correctly followed. Verse 35 preserves the most famous agraphon (unrecorded saying) of Jesus.

All disagreements -- whether in word choice, sentence structure, or interpretive expansion -- are logged comprehensively below.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 1 | "the uproar ceased" | "the uproar had ceased" | "the uproar finally died down" | "the uproar had ceased" | LOGOS pluperfect captures temporal sequence; PNEUMA too colloquial |
| 1 | "having sent-for the disciples and having exhorted [them], having embraced [them], departed to journey into Macedonia" | "having sent for the disciples and having exhorted them, took leave of them and departed to go into Macedonia" | "called the disciples together, embraced them with words of encouragement, said his goodbyes, and set out for Macedonia" | "having sent for the disciples and having exhorted them, embraced them and departed to go into Macedonia" | Preserve participial chain; LOGOS "took leave" is interpretive for ΑΣΠΑΣΑΜΕΝΟΣ (embraced); EMET "journey" is awkward; retain "embraced" as literal |
| 2 | "those parts" | "those regions" | "those regions" | "those regions" | LOGOS/PNEUMA majority; "regions" is more natural English for ΜΕΡΗ |
| 2 | "with much speech" | "with many words" | "pouring out encouragement everywhere he went" | "with many words" | EMET is acceptable but LOGOS clearer; PNEUMA is a paraphrase not warranted by Greek |
| 2 | "he came into Greece" | "he came into Greece" | "eventually arrived in Greece" | "he came into Greece" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "eventually" without textual basis |
| 3 | "a plot having been made against him" | "when a plot was formed against him" | "he discovered a Jewish plot against him" | "when a plot was formed against him" | LOGOS best renders the genitive absolute; PNEUMA makes Paul the discoverer (not in Greek) |
| 3 | "[a] decision came-about to return" | "he determined to return" | "he decided to go back" | "he decided to return" | EMET is wooden; LOGOS/PNEUMA both capture ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ ΓΝΩΜΗΣ as a decision; "go back" is too informal |
| 4 | "Sopater [son] of Pyrrhus, a Berean" | "Sopater of Berea, the son of Pyrrhus" | "Sopater son of Pyrrhus from Berea" | "Sopater son of Pyrrhus, a Berean" | EMET word order closest to Greek; retain "Berean" as adjectival form matching Sinaiticus ΒΕΡΟΙΑΙΟΣ |
| 4 | "of [the] Asians" | "of Asia" | "from the province of Asia" | "of Asia" | LOGOS is clean; PNEUMA adds "province" (interpretive); EMET "Asians" is awkward |
| 5 | "were waiting for us in Troas" | "were waiting for us at Troas" | "were waiting for us in Troas" | "were waiting for us at Troas" | LOGOS "at" better for a city destination in English |
| 6 | "sailed-out from Philippi" | "sailed from Philippi" | "sailed from Philippi" | "sailed from Philippi" | LOGOS/PNEUMA majority; "sailed-out" is unnecessary compound |
| 6 | "the days of Unleavened-Bread" | "the days of Unleavened Bread" | "the Festival of Unleavened Bread" | "the days of Unleavened Bread" | EMET/LOGOS agree on literal rendering; PNEUMA "Festival" is interpretive addition |
| 6 | "came to them in Troas" | "came to them at Troas" | "caught up with them in Troas" | "came to them at Troas" | LOGOS preposition; PNEUMA "caught up" adds implication not in Greek |
| 6 | "we stayed seven days" | "we stayed seven days" | "we stayed a week" | "we stayed seven days" | EMET/LOGOS literal; "a week" is fine but less precise |
| 7 | "we having been gathered-together" | "when we were gathered together" | "when we had gathered" | "when we had gathered together" | Blend: LOGOS temporal clause + PNEUMA active voice captures meaning; EMET participial is awkward in English |
| 7 | "Paul was discoursing to them" | "Paul was discoursing with them" | "Paul spoke to them" | "Paul was speaking to them" | "discoursing" is archaic; "spoke" (PNEUMA) loses imperfective aspect; "speaking" preserves ongoing action |
| 7 | "he was extending the speech" | "he prolonged his speech" | "he kept talking" | "he prolonged his speech" | LOGOS most precise; PNEUMA too casual; EMET awkward |
| 8 | "sufficient lamps" | "many lamps" | "plenty of lamps burning" | "many lamps" | Greek ΙΚΑΝΑΙ means "sufficient/considerable/many"; LOGOS standard; PNEUMA adds "burning" (not in text) |
| 8 | "the upper-room where we were gathered-together" | "the upper room where we were gathered together" | "the upstairs room where we were meeting" | "the upper room where we were gathered together" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "upstairs room" and "meeting" are interpretive |
| 9 | "sitting upon the window" | "sitting in the window" | "sitting in the window" | "sitting in the window" | LOGOS/PNEUMA majority; "upon" is less natural in English |
| 9 | "being borne-down by a deep sleep" | "was being overcome by deep sleep" | "sank deeper and deeper into sleep" | "was being overcome by deep sleep" | LOGOS captures ΚΑΤΑΦΕΡΟΜΕΝΟΣ well; PNEUMA is vivid paraphrase; EMET "borne-down" is awkward |
| 9 | "while Paul discoursed yet-longer" | "as Paul continued speaking at length" | "As Paul talked on and on" | "as Paul continued speaking at length" | LOGOS best balance; PNEUMA colloquial; EMET archaic |
| 9 | "was taken-up dead" | "was taken up dead" | "was picked up dead" | "was taken up dead" | EMET/LOGOS agree; "picked up" (PNEUMA) is too casual for ΗΡΘΗ |
| 10 | "having gone-down, fell-upon him and having embraced [him]" | "went down and fell upon him, and having embraced him" | "went down, threw himself on the young man, and wrapped his arms around him" | "went down and fell upon him, and having embraced him" | LOGOS preserves Greek structure; PNEUMA dramatically expands; EMET hyphenation awkward |
| 10 | "Do not be troubled" | "Do not be troubled" | "Stop panicking!" | "Do not be troubled" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "panicking" is too strong for ΘΟΡΥΒΕΙΣΘΕ |
| 10 | "for his life is in him" | "for his life is in him" | "He's alive!" | "for his life is in him" | EMET/LOGOS preserve Paul's precise Greek wording; PNEUMA collapses the statement |
| 11 | "having tasted" | "eaten" | "ate" | "eaten" | LOGOS/PNEUMA render ΓΕΥΣΑΜΕΝΟΣ naturally; EMET "tasted" is hyper-literal |
| 11 | "having conversed for a considerable-time until dawn, thus he departed" | "having conversed at length until dawn, so he departed" | "He went on talking until dawn broke, and then he left" | "having conversed at length until dawn, so he departed" | LOGOS best preserves participial structure; PNEUMA restructures entirely |
| 12 | "they brought the boy alive" | "they brought the boy alive" | "they took him home alive" | "they brought the boy alive" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "home" (not in Greek) |
| 12 | "were comforted not moderately" | "were exceedingly comforted" | "the relief they felt was beyond words" | "were greatly comforted" | ΟΥ ΜΕΤΡΙΩΣ (not moderately) is litotes; "exceedingly" (LOGOS) is strong; "greatly" captures litotes without overstatement; PNEUMA is a paraphrase |
| 13 | "from-there being-about to take-up Paul" | "intending from there to take Paul on board" | "where we planned to pick up Paul" | "intending from there to take Paul on board" | LOGOS most natural rendering; PNEUMA "pick up" too casual; EMET awkward |
| 13 | "being-about himself to go-on-foot" | "intending himself to go on foot" | "he intended to go there on foot" | "intending himself to go on foot" | EMET/LOGOS agree in substance; PNEUMA adds "there" |
| 14 | "having taken him up" | "we took him on board" | "we took him aboard" | "we took him on board" | LOGOS clearest; PNEUMA acceptable; EMET literal but less clear |
| 15 | "on the following [day] we arrived opposite Chios" | "on the following day we arrived opposite Chios" | "we arrived the next day off Chios" | "on the following day we arrived opposite Chios" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "off" is less precise for ΑΝΤΙΚΡΥΣ |
| 15 | "on the next [day] we crossed-over to Samos" | "the next day we crossed over to Samos" | "The day after that we crossed over to Samos" | "the next day we crossed over to Samos" | All agree in substance; LOGOS cleanest expression |
| 15 | "on the following [day] we came to Miletus" | "the day after we came to Miletus" | "the following day reached Miletus" | "the day after we came to Miletus" | LOGOS/PNEUMA agree on "day after"; PNEUMA drops "we came" |
| 16 | "so-that it might not happen to him to spend-time in Asia" | "so that he might not have to spend time in Asia" | "so he wouldn't lose time in the province of Asia" | "so that he might not have to spend time in Asia" | LOGOS renders purpose clause naturally; PNEUMA adds "province" and "lose" (interpretive) |
| 16 | "if it were possible for him" | "if it were possible for him" | "if at all possible" | "if it were possible for him" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA drops "for him" |
| 17 | "he summoned the elders of the assembly" | "he summoned the elders of the church" | "he called for the elders of the church" | "he summoned the elders of the church" | LOGOS/PNEUMA "church" for ΕΚΚΛΗΣΙΑ is standard; EMET "assembly" is literal but inconsistent with common usage; "summoned" over "called for" |
| 18 | "You yourselves know" | "You yourselves know" | "You know" | "You yourselves know" | EMET/LOGOS preserve emphatic ΥΜΕΙΣ; PNEUMA drops emphasis |
| 18 | "on which I set-foot in Asia" | "on which I set foot in Asia" | "I set foot in Asia" | "on which I set foot in Asia" | All agree; EMET/LOGOS preserve relative clause structure |
| 19 | "with all humility and tears and trials" | "with all humility and with tears and with trials" | "with complete humility, through tears and through the ordeals" | "with all humility and with tears and with trials" | LOGOS preserves prepositional parallelism; PNEUMA "complete" and "ordeals" are interpretive |
| 19 | "the [ones] having come-upon me by the plots of the Jews" | "that came upon me through the plots of the Jews" | "that came crashing down on me from Jewish conspiracies" | "that came upon me through the plots of the Jews" | LOGOS natural; PNEUMA "crashing down" and "conspiracies" are dramatic additions |
| 20 | "I held-back nothing of the [things] profitable [so as] not to declare to you and to teach you" | "I did not shrink from declaring to you anything that was profitable, and teaching you" | "I never held anything back that would help you. I taught you" | "I did not shrink from declaring to you anything that was profitable, and teaching you" | LOGOS restructures the double negative clearly; PNEUMA splits into two sentences and uses "help" for "profitable" |
| 20 | "publicly and from house-to-house" | "publicly and from house to house" | "publicly and went house to house" | "publicly and from house to house" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds verb "went" |
| 21 | "testifying both to Jews and to Greeks repentance toward God and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ" | "testifying both to Jews and to Greeks of repentance toward God and faith in our Lord Jesus Christ" | "urging both Jews and Greeks to turn to God in repentance and to put their trust in our Lord Jesus" | "testifying both to Jews and to Greeks of repentance toward God and of faith in our Lord Jesus Christ" | LOGOS adds "of" for clarity; PNEUMA "urging" replaces "testifying" (ΔΙΑΜΑΡΤΥΡΟΜΕΝΟΣ); PNEUMA drops "Christ"; EMET "toward" for faith less idiomatic than "in" |
| 22 | "I, having been bound in the Spirit, am journeying to Jerusalem" | "I am going to Jerusalem, bound in the Spirit" | "I am going to Jerusalem, bound in my spirit" | "I am going to Jerusalem, bound in the Spirit" | LOGOS word order best; PNEUMA "my spirit" is interpretive (Greek is ambiguous ΤΩ ΠΝΕΥΜΑΤΙ); EMET participial placement is awkward |
| 22 | "the [things] in it [that will] meet me not knowing" | "not knowing what will happen to me there" | "not knowing what will happen to me there" | "not knowing what will happen to me there" | LOGOS/PNEUMA far clearer; EMET is unintelligible in English |
| 23 | "the Holy Spirit in every city testifies to me, saying that bonds and afflictions await me" | "the Holy Spirit testifies to me in every city, saying that bonds and afflictions await me" | "in city after city the Holy Spirit keeps warning me that chains and suffering are waiting for me" | "the Holy Spirit testifies to me in every city, saying that bonds and afflictions await me" | LOGOS word order natural; PNEUMA "warning" for ΔΙΑΜΑΡΤΥΡΕΤΑΙ is interpretive, "chains" for ΔΕΣΜΑ acceptable but "bonds" more literal, "suffering" for ΘΛΙΨΕΙΣ less precise |
| 24 | "I make of no account [my] life [as] precious to myself" | "I consider my life of no account as dear to myself" | "I don't consider my life worth anything to myself" | "I consider my life of no account as precious to myself" | Blend LOGOS structure with EMET "precious" (ΤΙΜΙΑΝ); PNEUMA omits the "precious/dear" qualifier |
| 24 | "so-that I might finish my course" | "so that I may finish my course" | "if only I can finish the race" | "so that I may finish my course" | LOGOS subjunctive natural; PNEUMA "race" is interpretive for ΔΡΟΜΟΝ (course/race); EMET acceptable |
| 24 | "the ministry which I received from the Lord Jesus" | "the ministry which I received from the Lord Jesus" | "the mission the Lord Jesus gave me" | "the ministry which I received from the Lord Jesus" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "mission" for ΔΙΑΚΟΝΙΑ is interpretive |
| 24 | "to testify the good-news of the grace of God" | "to testify to the gospel of the grace of God" | "to be a witness to the good news of God's grace" | "to testify to the gospel of the grace of God" | LOGOS best; EMET "good-news" acceptable; PNEUMA restructures genitive chain |
| 25 | "no-longer will you see my face, all of you among whom I went-about proclaiming the kingdom" | "all of you, among whom I went about proclaiming the kingdom, will see my face no more" | "none of you among whom I went about proclaiming the kingdom will ever see my face again" | "all of you, among whom I went about proclaiming the kingdom, will see my face no more" | LOGOS word order superior; PNEUMA "ever...again" is emphatic addition; all correctly preserve "the kingdom" without "of God" per Sinaiticus |
| 26 | "I testify to you on this very day that I [am] clean from the blood of all" | "I testify to you on this day that I am innocent of the blood of all" | "I declare to you today -- I am clean of everyone's blood" | "I testify to you on this day that I am innocent of the blood of all" | LOGOS "innocent" better English for ΚΑΘΑΡΟΣ in legal context; PNEUMA "declare" for ΜΑΡΤΥΡΟΜΑΙ acceptable; EMET "this very day" emphatic but "this day" sufficient |
| 27 | "I did not shrink-back from declaring to you all the counsel of God" | "I did not shrink from declaring to you the whole counsel of God" | "I never shrank back from telling you the whole plan of God" | "I did not shrink from declaring to you the whole counsel of God" | LOGOS preferred; PNEUMA "plan" for ΒΟΥΛΗ is interpretive (counsel is broader); "telling" less formal than "declaring" |
| 28 | "Take-heed to yourselves and to all the flock" | "Take heed to yourselves and to all the flock" | "Watch out for yourselves and for the entire flock" | "Take heed to yourselves and to all the flock" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "watch out" is colloquial, "entire" for ΠΑΝΤΙ acceptable |
| 28 | "the Holy Spirit placed you [as] overseers" | "the Holy Spirit has made you overseers" | "the Holy Spirit has placed in your care" | "the Holy Spirit has placed you as overseers" | Blend EMET "placed" (ΕΘΕΤΟ) with LOGOS structure; PNEUMA omits "overseers" (ΕΠΙΣΚΟΠΟΥΣ) as separate term |
| 28 | "to shepherd the assembly of God" | "to shepherd the church of God" | "Shepherd the church of God" | "to shepherd the church of God" | LOGOS/PNEUMA "church"; EMET "assembly" -- "church" is standard for ΕΚΚΛΗΣΙΑ |
| 28 | "which he obtained through the blood of his own [Son]" | "which he obtained through his own blood" | "which he purchased with his own blood" | "which he obtained through the blood of his own" | Critical variant. EMET supplies "[Son]" to resolve ambiguity. LOGOS reads "his own blood" (could imply God's blood). PNEUMA "purchased" for ΠΕΡΙΕΠΟΙΗΣΑΤΟ is slightly interpretive. SOPHIA retains "the blood of his own" preserving the Greek ambiguity of ΤΟΥ ΙΔΙΟΥ without resolving it |
| 29 | "grievous wolves" | "fierce wolves" | "savage wolves" | "fierce wolves" | LOGOS/PNEUMA both strong; "fierce" for ΒΑΡΕΙΣ is most precise; EMET "grievous" is archaic |
| 30 | "from among your own selves will arise men speaking distorted-things" | "from among your own selves men will arise, speaking distorted things" | "from your own ranks, men will rise up and twist the truth" | "from among your own selves men will arise, speaking distorted things" | LOGOS word order; PNEUMA "twist the truth" is interpretive expansion for ΔΙΕΣΤΡΑΜΜΕΝΑ; "ranks" for context acceptable but "selves" more literal |
| 30 | "to draw-away the disciples after themselves" | "to draw away the disciples after themselves" | "to drag disciples away after them" | "to draw away the disciples after themselves" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "drag" is more forceful than ΑΠΟΣΠΑΝ warrants |
| 31 | "be-watchful" | "be watchful" | "stay alert" | "be watchful" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA acceptable synonym |
| 31 | "I did not cease admonishing each one" | "I did not cease admonishing each one" | "I never stopped warning each one of you" | "I did not cease admonishing each one" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "warning" for ΝΟΥΘΕΤΩΝ less precise (admonishing includes instruction) |
| 31 | "with tears" | "with tears" | "often with tears" | "with tears" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA adds "often" (not in text) |
| 32 | "I commend you to God and to the word of his grace" | "I commend you to God and to the word of his grace" | "I hand you over to God and to the message of his grace" | "I commend you to God and to the word of his grace" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA "hand you over" and "message" are interpretive |
| 32 | "the [one] being able to build-up and to give the inheritance among all the [ones] having been sanctified" | "which is able to build you up and to give you the inheritance among all those who are sanctified" | "which has the power to build you up and give you an inheritance among all those who are set apart for him" | "which is able to build you up and to give you the inheritance among all those who have been sanctified" | LOGOS clearest; PNEUMA "set apart for him" is interpretive expansion of ΗΓΙΑΣΜΕΝΟΙΣ; "has the power" acceptable variant of "is able"; EMET overly literal |
| 33 | "Silver or gold or clothing of no-one did I covet" | "I coveted no one's silver or gold or clothing" | "I never wanted anyone's silver or gold or clothing" | "I coveted no one's silver or gold or clothing" | LOGOS natural English word order; PNEUMA "wanted" is weaker than ΕΠΕΘΥΜΗΣΑ (coveted) |
| 34 | "these hands ministered to my needs and to the [ones] being with me" | "these hands ministered to my own needs and to those who were with me" | "these hands of mine provided for my own needs and for the needs of those who were with me" | "these hands ministered to my own needs and to those who were with me" | LOGOS clean; PNEUMA adds "of mine" and repeats "needs" (not in Greek); EMET "the ones being with me" is awkward |
| 35 | "thus laboring, it is necessary to support the [ones] being weak" | "by laboring in this way it is necessary to help the weak" | "by working hard like this we must support the weak" | "by laboring in this way it is necessary to help the weak" | LOGOS natural; PNEUMA "working hard" for ΚΟΠΙΩΝΤΑΣ is interpretive; "must" for ΔΕΙ acceptable |
| 35 | "to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, that he himself said" | "to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, that he himself said" | "we must remember the words the Lord Jesus himself spoke" | "to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, that he himself said" | EMET/LOGOS agree; PNEUMA restructures and adds "we must" |
| 35 | "'It is more blessed to give than to receive'" | "'It is more blessed to give than to receive'" | "'It is a greater blessing to give than to receive'" | "'It is more blessed to give than to receive'" | EMET/LOGOS agree on standard rendering; PNEUMA "greater blessing" is interpretive rephrasing of ΜΑΚΑΡΙΟΝ |
| 36 | "having knelt with all of them, he prayed" | "he knelt down and prayed with them all" | "he knelt down with all of them and prayed" | "he knelt down with them all and prayed" | Blend: PNEUMA/LOGOS placement of "with them all"; EMET participial chain less natural |
| 37 | "considerable weeping of all came-about" | "there was much weeping among them all" | "They all broke down weeping" | "there was much weeping among them all" | LOGOS natural rendering; EMET "considerable" awkward; PNEUMA "broke down" is dramatic addition |
| 37 | "having fallen-upon the neck of Paul, they were kissing him" | "they fell upon Paul's neck and kissed him" | "They threw their arms around Paul's neck and kissed him" | "they fell upon Paul's neck and kissed him" | LOGOS faithful; PNEUMA "threw their arms" is interpretive expansion of ΕΠΙΠΕΣΟΝΤΕΣ |
| 38 | "grieving most-of-all over the word which he had spoken" | "grieving especially over the word that he had spoken" | "heartbroken most of all by his words" | "grieving most of all over the word that he had spoken" | EMET "most-of-all" = LOGOS "especially" both valid for ΜΑΛΙΣΤΑ; "most of all" chosen; PNEUMA "heartbroken" is interpretive, "words" plural not matching singular ΛΟΓΩ |
| 38 | "that they are-about no-longer to behold his face" | "that they would see his face no more" | "that they would never see his face again" | "that they would see his face no more" | LOGOS best English rendering; PNEUMA "never...again" adds emphasis; EMET present tense "are-about" is awkward |
| 38 | "they were escorting him to the ship" | "they accompanied him to the ship" | "they walked him to the ship" | "they accompanied him to the ship" | LOGOS dignified; PNEUMA "walked him" is too casual; EMET "escorting" slightly formal but acceptable |
